subject: Properties of Expectation
topic: §46 Expected Value of a Function of Two Random Variables
question: Suppose a box contains 10 green, 10 red and 10 black balls. We draw 10 balls from the box by sampling with replacement. Let $X$ be the number of green balls, and $Y$ be the number of black balls in the sample. (a) Find $E(XY)$. (b) Are $X$ and $Y$ independent? Explain.
explanation:
First we note that $X$ and $Y$ are binomial with $n = 10$ and $p =  rac{1}{3}$. (a) Let $X_i$ be 1 if we get a green ball on the $i$th draw and 0 otherwise, and $Y_j$ be the event that in $j$th draw we got a black ball. Trivially, $X_i$ and $Y_j$ are independent if $1 \le i 
e j \le 10$. Moreover, $X_iY_i = 0$ for all $1 \le i \le 10$. Since $X = X_1 + X_2 + \ldots X_{10}$ and $Y = Y_1 + Y_2 + \ldots Y_{10}$ we have $XY = \sum\sum_{1\le i
e j\le 10} X_iY_j$. Hence, $E(XY) = \sum\sum_{1\le i
e j\le 10} E(X_iY_j) = \sum\sum_{1\le i
e j\le 10} E(X_i)E(Y_j) = 90 	imes  rac{1}{3} 	imes  rac{1}{3} = 10$. (b) Since $E(X) = E(Y) =  rac{10}{3}$, we have $E(XY) 
e E(X)E(Y)$ so $X$ and $Y$ are dependent. The following inequality will be of importance in the next section. Proposition 46.6 (Markov's Inequality) If $X \ge 0$ and $c > 0$ then $\Pr(X \ge c) \le  rac{E(X)}{c}$. Proof. Let $c > 0$. Define $$I =  egin{cases} 1 & 	ext{if $X \ge c$} \ 0 & 	ext{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Since $X \ge 0$, we have $I \le  rac{X}{c}$. Taking expectations of both side we find $E(I) \le  rac{E(X)}{c}$. Now the result follows since $E(I) = \Pr(X \ge c)$
